topic: A neighbor starts a renovation project
raw_output:
[story 1]

The rhythmic thudding started precisely at seven in the morning, a sound that vibrated through the floorboards and settled deep in my chest. Every time the hammer struck the wood, my shoulders tightened, pulling up toward my ears. I kept glancing through the blinds, half-expecting to see the intrusion—the dust cloud, the lumber piled against Mrs. Henderson’s prize-winning rose bushes. I took a slow, shallow breath, trying to regulate the quickening pace of my heart. *It’s just renovation,* I told myself, the words sounding thin and inadequate against the relentless percussion. I fiddled with the loose thread on my sweater, my fingers tracing the pattern as if it held some secret reassurance. The sheer scale of the work, the noise, the sheer *change* happening right next door, made the air feel thick and hard to draw in.

[story 2]

I spent the entire afternoon staring at the freshly patched section of the shared fence. Each splinter, each uneven line of the new wood, seemed to mock my attempts at calm. My hands were perpetually damp, and I found myself compulsively checking the clock, the second hand ticking with exaggerated slowness. When Mr. Davies finally emerged, wiping sweat from his brow with the back of a gloved hand, he offered a bright, almost too-eager smile. "Morning!" he chirped. His voice was too loud, too buoyant for the heavy silence I was trying to maintain inside my own head. I managed a tight nod, my throat suddenly constricted. I focused intensely on the chipped paint on the windowsill, willing myself to become invisible, a statue carved from sheer stillness, hoping the commotion would simply pass over me unnoticed.

[story 3]

The smell of sawdust and fresh-cut pine had permeated the entire block by midday. It was a sharp, invasive scent that clung to my clothes and settled in the back of my throat, making swallowing feel like a deliberate effort. I retreated to the living room, curling into the armchair, my knees drawn up to my chest. My gaze kept darting toward the window, watching the construction site with a hyper-vigilant intensity, cataloging every movement—the swing of a tool, the scrape of a saw blade. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the ache spreading through my temples. If I didn't watch, if I didn't process every detail of this ongoing upheaval, I felt as though I might shatter. The silence between the hammering became unbearable, filled only with the internal drumming of my own rising tension.
